tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of JIA with a genetic etiology (LACC1-associated), enabling appropriate counseling and downstream management.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the term "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and explicitly notes a genetic cause (LACC1 defect/variant).
Explains at a basic level what JIA is (chronic inflammatory arthritis in children) and that symptoms can flare and remit.
Uses clear, age-appropriate/parent-appropriate language and checks understanding (e.g., asks for questions or teach-back).
Acknowledges uncertainty appropriately if any details are missing, but still commits to the most likely diagnosis consistent with the scenario.

Mentions treatment overview without prescribing (since L1), including that NSAIDs such as meloxicam are commonly used for symptom control, and that rheumatology-directed disease-modifying therapy may be needed depending on severity.

Provides safety-net advice: red flags requiring urgent care (fever with severe joint pain, inability to bear weight, rapidly worsening swelling/redness, signs of infection, new rash, eye pain/redness/vision changes).
Does not prescribe any medication in-tool for this L1 task; avoids giving dosing instructions as a definitive prescription.
If discussing medications hypothetically, prompts for allergy history and contraindications prior to any future prescribing (aligns with required safety checks).

Avoids misinformation: does not claim LACC1-associated JIA is infectious or caused by lifestyle; does not promise cure; does not recommend contraindicated therapies (e.g., routine systemic steroids without specialist context).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose LACC1-associated juvenile idiopathic arthritis and provide basic explanation and next-step guidance without prescribing.

steps: Elicit key inflammatory arthritis features (morning stiffness, swelling, duration) and impact on function.
Screen for systemic features and red flags (fever, severe focal pain, inability to bear weight, eye symptoms).

State and document diagnosis: juvenile idiopathic arthritis associated with LACC1 genetic defect.
Explain diagnosis in plain language, including chronicity, flares, and that it is inflammatory/autoimmune rather than infectious.
Outline general treatment approach (NSAIDs such as meloxicam for pain/inflammation; potential need for DMARD/biologic under rheumatology guidance) without prescribing.
Provide follow-up and safety-net guidance (when to seek urgent care; need for rheumatology and ophthalmology screening for uveitis as appropriate).

steps: Assess inflammatory arthritis symptoms and rule out urgent infection red flags.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly using the term "insomnia" and distinguishes it from normal short sleep or transient stress-related sleep difficulty.
Assesses insomnia features (sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), duration, frequency, daytime impairment, and precipitating/maintaining factors.

Screens for red flags and secondary causes that would change management (e.g., suicidality, mania/hypomania, substance use, severe depression/anxiety, pregnancy, uncontrolled pain, neurologic symptoms).

Screens for common sleep disorders that can mimic or worsen insomnia (obstructive sleep apnea symptoms such as snoring/witnessed apneas, restless legs symptoms/urge to move legs, circadian rhythm disorder, parasomnias).

Before prescribing, performs the required safety checks: asks about medication allergies (including prior reactions to zolpidem or other sedative-hypnotics), checks for drug-drug interactions (especially other CNS depressants such as opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, sedating antihistamines), and checks contraindications/precautions (history of complex sleep behaviors on hypnotics, severe hepatic impairment, older/fall risk, respiratory compromise/possible OSA, pregnancy/breastfeeding).
Explains treatment including both "CBT-I" as first-line therapy and "zolpidem" as a short-term option; provides rationale and sets expectations for benefits and limitations.

If zolpidem is used, counsels on safe use: lowest effective dose, short duration, take only when able to sleep a full night, avoid alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment risk, and warning about complex sleep behaviors; advises stopping and seeking care if these occur.

Provides sleep hygiene guidance aligned with CBT-I principles (stimulus control, consistent wake time, limiting time in bed, avoiding naps/caffeine late, reducing evening screen time) without presenting sleep hygiene as the sole treatment.
Creates a clear follow-up plan (timeframe, response criteria, adverse effects monitoring, and when to seek urgent care), even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia; recommend CBT-I as first-line and prescribe short-term zolpidem after completing allergy/interaction/contraindication checks and counseling.
steps: Elicit insomnia phenotype, duration, frequency, and daytime impact; identify precipitating/maintaining factors and sleep schedule.
Screen for secondary causes and competing sleep disorders (OSA, RLS, circadian issues) and assess psychiatric/substance contributors and safety (suicidality).
Explain diagnosis explicitly as insomnia and briefly outline why other causes are less likely based on screening.
Recommend CBT-I (or CBT-I referral/resources) as first-line and outline key components (stimulus control, sleep restriction, consistent wake time).

Complete required safety checks before prescribing: allergies, drug interactions (CNS depressants), and contraindications/precautions (complex sleep behaviors, hepatic impairment, respiratory risk/possible OSA, older adult fall risk).
Prescribe zolpidem as a short-term adjunct when appropriate; counsel on dosing timing, avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives, next-day impairment, complex sleep behaviors, and limited duration.
Set follow-up timeframe to reassess efficacy, adverse effects, and need for ongoing therapy; provide return precautions.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as chikungunya and links it to mosquito-borne transmission (must include the words "chikungunya" and "mosquito").

Screens for danger signs and alternate diagnoses requiring escalation (e.g., hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, severe bleeding, pregnancy, extremes of age, significant comorbidities).

If any medication is proposed, performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about drug allergies (critical), contraindications (critical; e.g., pregnancy status, liver disease for acetaminophen, bleeding risk/possible dengue for NSAIDs/aspirin), and drug interactions (non-critical).

Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not recommend aspirin/NSAIDs before dengue is reasonably excluded; does not suggest antibiotics or antivirals as routine therapy for uncomplicated chikungunya.
Provides clear return precautions and follow-up guidance for worsening symptoms, dehydration, persistent severe joint pain, bleeding, or new neurologic symptoms.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "hereditary stomatocytosis" and explicitly links it to "hemolysis" (chronic red cell breakdown) in patient-friendly language.

Elicits key history for hemolysis severity and complications (fatigue/exercise tolerance, dark urine, jaundice, abdominal pain suggestive of gallstones, prior transfusions, family history).

Recommends appropriate evaluation/monitoring concepts (CBC with indices, reticulocyte count, bilirubin/LDH/haptoglobin, peripheral smear, assessment for iron overload if transfused) while not requiring any specific READ tool usage.

Avoids inappropriate definitive therapy suggestions for hereditary stomatocytosis (e.g., does not recommend routine splenectomy as a default; acknowledges it can increase thrombotic risk in some hereditary stomatocytosis subtypes and should be specialist-directed).

Includes the required safety check behavior: before any prescribing, performs an allergy check (or explicitly asks about allergies); if ordering is discussed, considers contraindications where applicable (non-critical).
Provides clear return precautions for worsening anemia/hemolysis (syncope, chest pain, severe dyspnea, rapidly worsening jaundice, severe abdominal pain, signs of infection).
